narrative.executive_view: The selected opportunity is a recurring-revenue SMB managed-service provider (MSP), rather than all computer-systems-design businesses. AI is already being used to automate ticketing, patching, and monitoring, so it can remove meaningful delivery work. The economics remain evidence-limited: recurring per-user/per-device and bundle billing can initially retain savings, but smaller customer deals, price pressure, vendor costs, and an unmeasured seat-count effect can pass savings through. Consolidation creates a credible add-on-to-platform multiple path, while also making targets competitive. [S1][S3][S4]
narrative.target_archetype: The archetype is a US regional MSP serving SMB customers through recurring managed IT, endpoint, helpdesk, cybersecurity, backup, and advisory contracts, with enough adjusted EBITDA to fall in the supplied $1–10M band. It excludes custom-development shops, pure VAR/resale books, one-off project integrators, and outsourced operation of a client-owned data center. MSP surveys and deal reports are business-model samples that cross NAICS 541512, 541513, and 541519, so no source maps their share of the supplied NAICS band; the coverage range is deliberately an unsupported assumption rather than a claim about all 3,524 firms. [S1][S4]
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: The operational mechanism is specific to managed delivery: AI can triage and answer common tickets, draft or execute routine remediation, assist patching and monitoring, prepare documentation, and reduce project and account-management administration. Kaseya reports that 53% of surveyed MSPs already use AI for ticketing, patching, and monitoring, but more than half have automated only about one-quarter of workload. That supports partial rather than wholesale task replacement: architecture, client-environment knowledge, incident accountability, field work, sales, and trusted-advisor work remain. [S1]
narrative.value_capture: Near-term capture is possible because MSPs commonly bill with recurring per-user, per-device, tiered-bundle, or fixed-fee subscription structures rather than pure hourly labor. The billing structure can decouple a lower service-delivery cost from revenue through a contract period. It is not proof of 24-month capture: the same survey shows substantial month-to-month and one-year contracting, and the 2026 survey reports smaller typical customer spending and intensified customer-acquisition difficulty. The retained-savings range therefore explicitly allows competitive repricing, higher platform/vendor costs, and client headcount reductions to absorb much of the gross saving. [S1][S3]
narrative.adoption_timing: The available current survey is a global MSP proxy rather than a census of US lower-middle-market targets. It reports 53% already using AI to automate ticketing, patching, and monitoring and measurable operational improvements, which supports a base case that majority material use in these delivery tasks has been reached. Because more than half have automated only around a quarter of workload and the survey population is broader than the archetype, the range retains a two-year possibility for the target population rather than treating tool availability as complete operational adoption. [S1]
narrative.buyability: The supplied band has 3,524 firms before archetype coverage, but the actual MSP portion is unmeasured. Buyer competition is plainly material: a 2025 MSP deal report records 466 deals, roughly 85% PE participation, 16 Evergreen MSP acquisitions in Q4, and transactions by Thrive, The 20 MSP, HarborIT, Corporate Tech, and Omega. The seller-supply signal is kept MISSING because no reopened source measures succession-driven supply for the selected US target tier; the record does not infer it from the deal wave or founder anecdotes. [S4][S7][S8]
narrative.valuation: The valuation bridge is conditional on buying a recurring-service add-on rather than a resale-heavy or project-heavy systems integrator. Solganick reports 5x–8x EBITDA for smaller MSP add-ons and 11x–20x for $10M-plus EBITDA platforms; Aventis independently describes 5x–8x for smaller MSPs while warning that its disclosed-transaction median reflects much larger deals and public comparables are imperfect. These are market proxies, not a measured entry or exit quote for this target archetype, so the ranges are broad and do not assume a realized spread. [S4][S5]
narrative.operator_survival: The mature outcome is assessed as PRESSURED, not as disappearance of client IT demand. Cybersecurity and backup remain important revenue lines, and Kaseya says 71% of respondents reported year-over-year cybersecurity revenue growth; ScalePad reports compliance-oriented MSPs have higher ARPU and recurring revenue. Those facts support a continuing accountable service role, particularly for security and compliance. Pressure is still substantial because vendors are embedding automation, only 13% of MSPs report meaningful revenue from AI services despite client demand, and the routine helpdesk/endpoint layer can be automated or bundled upstream. [S1][S6]
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest uncertainties are the share of the deterministic NAICS band that is genuinely recurring MSP, seller availability, and the 24-month share of AI savings retained after renewals and vendor pricing. MSP data are mostly global business-model surveys rather than six-digit US statistics, while the deterministic labor and firm-count inputs are NAICS based. A resale-heavy, project-heavy, or owner-dependent operator can have materially different margins, contract portability, and valuation from the selected archetype. The survey evidence also does not quantify AI-driven changes in client seat counts, so it cannot establish whether per-seat billing protects or shrinks the revenue base. [S1][S3][S4]

sources.title: Kaseya 2026 State of the MSP Report release
sources.what_it_supports: Current global MSP survey: 53% use AI for ticketing, patching, and monitoring; more than half have automated about one-quarter of workload; customer-spend, AI-service-revenue, cybersecurity-revenue, and competition indicators.

sources.title: How MSPs Are Reshaping Their Business in 2025
sources.what_it_supports: Prior global MSP benchmark proxy: about 30% said AI eliminated repetitive tasks and 20% said it created more time for strategy; used only as a directional adoption comparison.

sources.title: 2024 MSP Benchmark Survey Report
sources.what_it_supports: Survey evidence on per-user, per-device, tiered-bundle, and fixed-fee subscription billing and contract terms; supports the pricing-structure proxy but not a measured 24-month AI-savings retention rate.

sources.title: Technology Services M&A Market Update — Q4 2025 and 2026 Forecast
sources.what_it_supports: MSP deal volume, PE participation, named active acquirers, and reported smaller-add-on versus $10M-plus platform EBITDA-multiple ranges.

sources.title: MSP Valuation Multiples
sources.what_it_supports: Independent advisory proxy for small-MSP 5x–8x EBITDA range and limitations of disclosed and public comparable valuations.

sources.title: 2026 MSP Trends Report — Financial Performance
sources.what_it_supports: Survey proxy that compliance-oriented MSPs report higher ARPU and recurring revenue, supporting a durable but limited security/compliance accountability channel.

sources.title: Evergreen Celebrates 100th MSP Acquisition
sources.what_it_supports: Primary confirmation that Evergreen actively acquires MSPs and uses a decentralized continuing-operation model.

sources.title: Integris Announces Intent to Acquire First Focus
sources.what_it_supports: Current primary confirmation that a PE-backed SMB-focused MSP platform is pursuing acquisition and AI/security expansion.

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541512 ($168,217,056,007) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541512 ($278,318,454,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel
Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $341,141 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541512); class <5: 37,887 firms, avg revenue/firm $394,508 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $61,740 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 4,632 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,847,390 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $289,117 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 2,780 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,880,864 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $607,355 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 2,795 firms, avg revenue/firm $11,906,109 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,863,306 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 729 firms, avg revenue/firm $51,131,923 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $8,002,146 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 429 firms, avg revenue/firm $404,366,175 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $63,283,306 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 3,524
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: Current MSP survey evidence directly identifies ticketing, patching, and monitoring as AI-automated delivery tasks; the supplied 4-digit occupation mix is broader than the MSP archetype, so each task-level fraction is a bounded proxy judgment and excludes adoption, pricing, and deal evidence.
caveats: The supplied 5415 occupation mix understates a support-desk-heavy MSP mix and includes adjacent systems-design business models.
Fractions measure work potentially removed or materially compressed, not gross headcount reduction or revenue retention.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: AI can accelerate scripting, integration glue, configuration, documentation, and routine remediation, while solution architecture and client-specific environment judgment remain.
Ticket triage, common answers, password and onboarding workflows, and initial remediation map directly to AI-enabled service-desk automation; field and escalated support remain.
Reporting, prioritization support, and operational coordination compress, but responsibility for service quality, incidents, staffing, and client advice remains.
Assessment drafts, migration planning, documentation, and analysis can compress, while stakeholder discovery and architecture choices persist.

breakdown_by_role.role: Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Proposal drafting, CRM administration, and renewal paperwork automate in part; relationship selling and commercial negotiation remain.
Internal reporting and scheduling can compress, but owner relationships, capital allocation, and accountable operating judgment remain.
The heterogeneous technical tail includes automation-friendly monitoring and configuration work as well as specialist and physical work; the range reflects that uncertainty.
Status reporting, scheduling, risk logs, and meeting preparation compress, while client coordination and accountability persist.
Gap-analysis drafts and research compress, but advisory judgment, change management, and vendor selection remain.

breakdown_by_role.role: Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Test generation, log review, and routine validation are AI-exposed, while validation in unique customer environments and release accountability remain.
The unlisted wage tail plausibly contains dispatch, finance, procurement, additional support, and field technicians; automation is meaningful but bounded by physical and client-facing work.

target_archetype.name: Regional recurring-revenue SMB managed-service providers
inclusion_criteria: US regional MSPs with recurring managed IT, endpoint, helpdesk, cybersecurity, backup, or vCIO/advisory contracts serving SMB customers.
Businesses plausibly within the supplied $1–10M EBITDA band after adjusted-EBITDA normalization and with a service-led rather than resale-led revenue base.
exclusion_criteria: Custom software-development shops and project-only systems integrators.
Pure hardware/software resellers, businesses dominated by one-off implementation projects, and outsourced operation of client-owned facilities.
target_archetype.selection_basis: Recurring managed delivery is the economically coherent, actively consolidated archetype within this heterogeneous NAICS code. Industry surveys and M&A reports identify MSPs by business model, not six-digit NAICS, so they support archetype definition but not a measured share of the supplied NAICS target band.

coverage.rationale: No reopened source maps recurring SMB MSPs to the deterministic $1–10M EBITDA population within NAICS 541512. The base and range are explicit bounded assumptions for this heterogeneous code, not an estimate derived from the full NAICS count.
caveats: The coverage estimate needs a NAICS-coded establishment sample, business-description tagging, or a target screen before it can be treated as evidence.

retained_savings_share_24m.rationale: Recurring per-user, per-device, bundle, and fixed-fee contracts can retain lower delivery cost during a contract period, but the 2026 survey shows smaller customer spending and strong competition. The range is a proxy judgment for the 24-month net share after repricing, vendor charges, and client seat-count effects; neither source directly measures it.
caveats: No reopened source quantifies gross AI savings, renewal repricing, vendor pass-through, or client-seat changes for the selected US target tier.
The value is distinct from whether an MSP remains a paid operator at mature adoption.

t50_years.rationale: Kaseya's global MSP survey reports 53% already using AI to automate ticketing, patching, and monitoring, so the base case is that 50% material operational use in the relevant delivery tasks has already been reached. The high bound allows for a slower US lower-middle-market archetype than the survey population.
caveats: The survey does not isolate the US, the $1–10M EBITDA band, or a common threshold for material workload share.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: The observed 53% is the survey-reported share of MSPs using AI to automate ticketing, patching, and monitoring. The range is a bridge for survey uncertainty and the global-to-US-lower-middle-market mismatch.
caveats: It records operational use in named tasks, not the share of total workload automated or the share of each target's revenue exposed.

historical_analogs.value: Kaseya's 2025 benchmark said about 30% of MSPs used AI to eliminate repetitive tasks, while its 2026 survey reports 53% using AI for ticketing, patching, and monitoring. The questions and samples are not shown to be identical, so this is directional evidence of rapid workflow adoption rather than a calibrated adoption curve.
historical_analogs.rationale: Two annual industry-survey releases provide a business-model proxy for adoption direction but not a longitudinal target-archetype measurement.
caveats: Differences in survey wording, respondents, and definitions preclude calculating a precise adoption rate from the two percentages.

seller_supply_signal.rationale: No reopened source measures owner age, succession supply, or seller-to-buyer balance for US regional recurring SMB MSPs in the stated EBITDA band. A high deal count does not establish excess seller supply.
caveats: A target-level broker pipeline or owner-demographic study is required to replace UNKNOWN.

active_consolidators.rationale: A conservative public-buyer count is based on six distinct MSP acquirers named in Solganick's 2025 MSP transaction table: Evergreen, HarborIT, Corporate Tech, Thrive, The 20 MSP, and Omega. Evergreen and Integris primary announcements corroborate continuing platform acquisition activity. The 4–10 range recognizes that the table is not a US regional target-tier census and omits other credible bidders.
caveats: The number is a count of demonstrably or plausibly active platform buyers, not a full count of all PE sponsors, strategic buyers, or brokers.

buy_mult.rationale: The base and range align with reported 5x–8x smaller-MSP add-on ranges. They are a proxy for an MRR-weighted, transferable target and should not be applied to a resale-heavy, project-heavy, or owner-dependent operator without direct transaction evidence.
caveats: No reopened source discloses an entry multiple for a matched US regional target in the exact $1–10M EBITDA band.

exit_mult.rationale: The range uses Solganick's reported 11x–20x range for $10M-plus EBITDA MSP platforms, with the base near its lower half. It is a possible scaled-platform proxy, not a prediction that every roll-up earns a platform exit or that the selected target can reach the required scale and quality.
caveats: Platform multiples are not directly comparable to add-ons; exit requires integration, retention, recurring revenue quality, and buyer-market conditions not established here.

pricing_structure.value: Hybrid recurring billing: per-user and per-device fees, tiered managed-service bundles, and fixed-fee subscriptions, with some month-to-month and one-year contracts plus project/resale revenue.
pricing_structure.rationale: Kaseya's MSP survey documents these predominant billing alternatives and contract terms. The selected archetype requires a recurring-service-led mix, so the survey is a business-model proxy rather than a measured contract book for a target.
caveats: A target screen must verify MRR mix, renewal dates, pass-through clauses, and exposure to client user/device counts.

operator_survival.rationale: Current survey proxies support a continuing paid role in cybersecurity, backup, compliance, and governed client support, while the same evidence shows rapid automation of routine delivery and weak monetization of AI services. The resulting base class is PRESSURED: the operator can remain accountable, but routine support can migrate to vendors or self-service platforms.
caveats: No long-horizon evidence directly measures whether SMBs retain an independent MSP versus consume vendor-managed automation.
This mature-demand assessment is separate from the 24-month retained-savings estimate.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_timing_consistent: The task fractions describe technical delivery work that AI can remove or compress; the near-zero t50 base is supported separately by current MSP operational-use survey evidence. No adoption statistic was used to increase a role fraction.
cross_checks.capture_vs_survival_separated: Capture estimates the portion of gross operating savings retained at approximately 24 months under contract and renewal economics. Survival instead asks whether an independent MSP remains a paid accountable operator at mature automation; cybersecurity/compliance demand can support survival even if price pressure reduces early capture.
cross_checks.no_deal_evidence_in_VA: M&A volume, active buyer evidence, and valuation evidence are used only for buyability and multiples. Role fractions use only operational AI-task evidence and do not incorporate deal activity, pricing, or terminal demand.
cross_checks.multiple_neutrality_acknowledged: A flat entry-to-exit multiple is economically neutral under v4 because operating value is assessed separately; the supplied ranges are possible market proxies, not a claim that multiple expansion is necessary or certain.

dataset_mismatch.description: The deterministic data are US NAICS 541512 and the supplied role mix is four-digit 5415, whereas the research evidence is mostly global MSP business-model surveys and MSP deal reports. MSPs can be classified in neighboring computer-services codes, and the deterministic target band contains non-MSP systems-design firms.
sensitivity: Archetype coverage is an unsupported 0.20–0.50 assumption rather than a NAICS-derived count.
Support and field-work composition may differ materially from the supplied four-digit role mix.
Survey-based adoption, pricing, and survival evidence should not be read as NAICS-wide evidence.

subsegments: Recurring SMB managed service providers and managed security providers
Systems integration and implementation project firms
Outsourced client-facility operations and other computer services
market_boundary.selection_basis: The recurring MSP subsegment is selected because its contract-led service model creates a defined short-term savings-capture mechanism and is demonstrably active in MSP M&A. The other subsegments have different AI exposure, billing, labor, and valuation economics and are not pooled into the archetype.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: Changes effective target density and buyability; no empirical NAICS-to-MSP mapping was found.
Directly changes value retained and could be reduced by renewals, vendor pricing, competition, or AI-driven client seat declines.
The direction of seller supply changes buyability and is intentionally MISSING pending target-tier succession evidence.

top_uncertain_inputs.range: 11x–20x EBITDA for a qualifying scaled platform proxy
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: Directly changes the valuation effect; the range is not a target-specific exit forecast.
